topic.what: A textbook review chapter on Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice) with particular focus on deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL), which stimulates mucus secretion and endogenous prostaglandin production to protect the gastric mucosa without the mineralocorticoid adverse effects associated with intact glycyrrhizin.
topic.how: Evidence synthesis from clinical and pre-clinical studies of licorice and DGL preparations; mechanisms of mucosal protection described (mucus gel stimulation, prostaglandin E2 induction, COX-2 modulation); clinical applications in peptic ulcer and gastritis reviewed; safety profile of DGL vs. glycyrrhizin-containing preparations compared.
topic.why_matters: DGL offers mucosal-protective benefits of licorice without the hypertension and hypokalaemia risks of glycyrrhizin, making it suitable for long-term use; understanding this separation of therapeutic and adverse pharmacology enables safer clinical and naturopathic application.

characteristics.what_it_measures: DGL's ability to increase gastric mucus secretion, assessed in clinical and animal studies
characteristics.value: Demonstrated mucosal gel thickening and increased mucus glycoprotein output
characteristics.evidence: Pre-clinical and early clinical studies; mechanistic assays of mucin secretion
characteristics.context: DGL form specifically; response may vary with preparation and dose

characteristics.what_it_measures: Induction of mucosal prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) by DGL, contributing to cytoprotection
characteristics.value: Significant PGE2 elevation demonstrated in gastric mucosal models
characteristics.evidence: In-vitro and animal studies of prostaglandin biosynthesis
characteristics.context: Mechanistic evidence; clinical correlation to symptom relief less directly established

characteristics.what_it_measures: Absence of mineralocorticoid adverse effects (hypertension, hypokalaemia, oedema) with DGL vs. intact licorice
characteristics.value: DGL lacks glycyrrhizin; mineralocorticoid risk effectively eliminated at standard DGL doses
characteristics.evidence: Pharmacological characterisation of glycyrrhizin removal; clinical safety reports
characteristics.context: Safety advantage well established; enables longer treatment duration

good_for: Long-term mucosal protective therapy in gastritis, peptic ulcer, and NSAID-induced GI injury
Patients who cannot tolerate glycyrrhizin-related adverse effects (hypertension, renal/adrenal conditions)
Naturopathic and integrative medicine GI protocols
Complementary mucosal protection alongside H. pylori eradication therapy
not_for: H. pylori eradication (DGL is not antimicrobial at standard doses)
Acute severe peptic ulcer requiring acid suppression and urgent antibiotic therapy
requires: Standardised DGL preparation with confirmed low glycyrrhizin content (<3%)
Chewable or lozenge formulation for oral mucosal and gastric surface contact
Integration with dietary and lifestyle measures for full gastroprotective benefit

novelty: Provides a clear mechanistic framework separating DGL's gastroprotective pharmacology (mucus + prostaglandins) from the adverse mineralocorticoid effects of intact glycyrrhizin
Synthesises naturopathic and pharmacological evidence in a single textbook chapter, bridging conventional and integrative gastroenterology
Establishes DGL as a rational long-term option for mucosal protection where PPIs or other drugs carry risk or are contraindicated
open_problems: Standardisation of DGL preparations across commercial products (glycyrrhizin content varies)
Head-to-head RCT of DGL vs. sucralfate or misoprostol for NSAID gastroprotection
Optimal dose and formulation (chewable vs. capsule vs. liquid) for maximum mucosal contact and PGE2 stimulation
Long-term clinical outcomes (ulcer recurrence, cancer risk) with DGL maintenance therapy
